APIs or of the language itself"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":20,"created_at":"2016-08-20T17:20:01Z","updated_at":"2024-11-04T00:36:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The situation for the `similar` function feels a little messy these days (perhaps I'm wrong about that and it's all under control, in which case, consider this a documentation request). It seems like the `similar` API could be clarified with appropriate traits, e.g.

``` jl
similar(a, Dense) # same dimensions and element type as `a` but dense
similar(a, Sparse) # same dimensions and element type as `a` but sparse
similar(a, Mutable) # same dimensions and element type as `a` but fully mutable
similar(a, ElType{T}) # same dimensions and storage type as `a` but with element type `T`
similar(a, Dimensions{...}) # same storage and element type as `a` but with different dimensions
```

This is sort of already what we're doing but the current system is a bit more ad hoc: type arguments are treated as an implicit element type trait and a tuple of integers is treated collectively as a dimensions trait. It might be fruitful to have a more explicit trait-based core API and translate the various shorthands into that.

It is not just useful for `AbstractArray` types but for `NTuple` or other types.

Case in point: I have a package which has its own implementation of `similar_type` for a non-array type, but does not need to depend on StaticArraysCore (the package that is usually relied on for this functionality). It would make sense for shared functionality to be in Base, IMO.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2453651158/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Julia manual explicitly notes that `Base.Broadcast.broadcasted(f, args...)` should be implemented](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/interfaces/#man-interfaces-broadcasting) when customizing the default behavior of broadcasting, but when the docstring is pulled up with REPL help, it mentions that the symbol is private:
```julia-repl
help?> Base.Broadcast.broadcasted
  │ Warning
  │
  │  The following bindings may be internal; they may change or be removed in future
  │  versions:
  │
  │    •  Base.Broadcast.broadcasted

  No documentation found for private symbol.

  Base.Broadcast.broadcasted is a Function.

  # 66 methods for generic function "broadcasted" from Base.Broadcast:
...
```
This is on Julia 1.11:
```julia-repl
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.11.1
Commit 8f5b7ca12ad (2024-10-16 10:53 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 8 × Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1065G7 CPU @ 1.30GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-16.0.6 (ORCJIT, icelake-client)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
```

release"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-02-18T22:11:19Z","updated_at":"2024-06-18T21:52:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!--
If you have a question please search or post to our Discourse site: https://discourse.julialang.org.
We use the GitHub issue tracker for bug reports and feature requests only.

If you're submitting a bug report, be sure to include as much relevant information as
possible, including:

1. The output of `versioninfo()`
2. How you installed Julia
3. A minimal working example (MWE), also known as a minimum reproducible example

If you're experiencing a problem with a particular package, open an issue on that
package's repository instead.

Thanks for contributing to the Julia project!
-->

If you have an abstract type with a constrained type parameter, subtypes whose parameters inherit from the type's parameters do not inherit the constraints.
```julia
abstract type AbstractFoo{T<:Real}
end

struct Foo{T} <: AbstractFoo{T} end
```

```julia-repl
julia> supertype(Foo)
AbstractFoo{T} where T

julia> supertype(Foo{<:Real}}
AbstractFoo

julia> Foo <: AbstractFoo    # This one is very much unexpected!
false

julia> Foo{<:Real} <: AbstractFoo
true
```
This is true even though you can't construct a type which violates the constraints on its parent's parameters:
```julia-repl
julia> Foo{String}
ERROR: TypeError: in AbstractFoo, in T, expected T<:Real, got Type{String}
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[5]:1
```
This is a subtle point that is not included in the documentation on abstract parametric types, but has caused me some headaches when some subtype relations broke unexpectedly.

The above examples were tested on Julia 1.10 (version info below) running on Arch Linux in WSL2, installed with the AUR provided `julia-bin` package that provides the official release:
```julia-repl
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.0
Commit 3120989f39b (2023-12-25 18:01 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 20 × 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13600K
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, goldmont)
  Threads: 1 on 20 virtual cores
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2177141283","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380#issuecomment-2177141283","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380","id":2177141283,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6BxIoj","user":{"login":"brainandforce","id":12368980,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMzY4OTgw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12368980?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce","html_url":"https://github.com/brainandforce","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-18T21:52:43Z","updated_at":"2024-06-18T21:52:43Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"@vtjnash {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380","id":2141208820,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5_oED0","number":53380,"title":"The behavior of type parameter constraint inheritance is not documented","user":{"login":"brainandforce","id":12368980,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMzY4OTgw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12368980?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce","html_url":"https://github.com/brainandforce","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-18T22:11:19Z","updated_at":"2024-02-18T22:11:19Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!--
If you have a question please search or post to our Discourse site: https://discourse.julialang.org.
We use the GitHub issue tracker for bug reports and feature requests only.

If you're submitting a bug report, be sure to include as much relevant information as
possible, including:

1. The output of `versioninfo()`
2. How you installed Julia
3. A minimal working example (MWE), also known as a minimum reproducible example

If you're experiencing a problem with a particular package, open an issue on that
package's repository instead.

Thanks for contributing to the Julia project!
-->

If you have an abstract type with a constrained type parameter, subtypes whose parameters inherit from the type's parameters do not inherit the constraints.
```julia
abstract type AbstractFoo{T<:Real}
end

struct Foo{T} <: AbstractFoo{T} end
```

```julia-repl
julia> supertype(Foo)
AbstractFoo{T} where T

julia> supertype(Foo{<:Real}}
AbstractFoo

julia> Foo <: AbstractFoo    # This one is very much unexpected!
false

julia> Foo{<:Real} <: AbstractFoo
true
```
This is true even though you can't construct a type which violates the constraints on its parent's parameters:
```julia-repl
julia> Foo{String}
ERROR: TypeError: in AbstractFoo, in T, expected T<:Real, got Type{String}
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[5]:1
```
This is a subtle point that is not included in the documentation on abstract parametric types, but has caused me some headaches when some subtype relations broke unexpectedly.

The above examples were tested on Julia 1.10 (version info below) running on Arch Linux in WSL2, installed with the AUR provided `julia-bin` package that provides the official release:
```julia-repl
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.0
Commit 3120989f39b (2023-12-25 18:01 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 20 × 13th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-13600K
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, goldmont)
  Threads: 1 on 20 virtual cores
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53380/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504","id":2157991653,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6AoFbl","number":53504,"title":"More correct inference for the return type of exponentiation","user":{"login":"brainandforce","id":12368980,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyMzY4OTgw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12368980?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce","html_url":"https://github.com/brainandforce","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/brainandforce/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-28T03:03:29Z","updated_at":"2024-02-28T03:03:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The function `Base.to_power_type(x)` is found [here](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/c379db77135e55b71707704f58d901e78924804d/base/intfuncs.jl#L298) and defined below:
```julia
to_power_type(x) = convert(Base._return_type(*, Tuple{typeof(x), typeof(x)}), x)
```
This function is used in `Base.power_by_squaring` to determine what type the result should be. I think the definition above is flawed, and we should be using this:
```julia
power_type(::Type{T}) where T = promote_type(Base._return_type(*, Tuple{T, T}), T, Base._return_type(one, Tuple{T}))

to_power_type(x::T) where T = convert(power_type(T), x)
```
# But why, you ask?

In the general case, to maintain type stability, the output of `Base.power_by_squaring(x, n)` needs to be a type that can store `x^0`, `x`, and `x^2`. However, `one(x)`, which is used to calculate `x^0`,  may be a different type from `x`:

> If possible, `one(x)` returns a value of the same type as `x`, and `one(T)` returns a value of type `T`. However, this may not be the case for types representing dimensionful quantities (e.g. time in days), since the multiplicative identity must be dimensionless. In that case, `one(x)` should return an identity value of the same precision (and shape, for matrices) as `x`.

I am currently writing a package, CliffordNumbers.jl, which has some data types `T` (such as `KVector{K}` when `K` is odd) for which `typeof(one(T))`, is not `T`, and on top of this, neither is `Base._return_type(*, Tuple{T, T})`!. This causes exponentiation to fail because the input is not representable as a `Base._return_type(*, Tuple{T, T})`.

The problem I faced might be a really niche edge case, but I think the way integer powers of types are handled in cases where they are not equivalent to the type itself is worth discussing and refining. The solution I present would completely solve my issues, but I'm not sure if it's too much of a breaking change.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53504/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}
